focus: joint Gate 3 x skill-advisor matrix plus rule-change dry-run simulation

summary: The 200-prompt corpus yields TT=73, TF=62, FT=34, and FF=31. Single-surface misses account for 96 of 127 total errors, while only 31 prompts fail both routers at once.

The TF bucket is dominated by already-correct Gate 3 file writes and packet-maintenance prompts whose gold skills are system-spec-kit (23) or sk-code-opencode (12). The main bad outputs are none (28), sk-improve-prompt (9), command-memory-save (5), command-spec-kit-resume (4), and mcp-chrome-devtools (5).

The FT bucket is mostly a Gate 3 recall problem: deep_loop_write contributes 22 prompts, resume_write contributes 2, and Gate 3 lands on no_match for 26 of the 34 FT rows while the advisor already selects the correct owning skill.

Threshold tuning is low leverage on the labeled corpus, while rule-surface cleanup is high leverage. The strongest isolated advisor proposal is command-surface normalization (accuracy 53.5% -> 60.0%), and the strongest isolated Gate 3 proposal is the three-part trigger bundle (F1 68.6 -> 89.0%).
Gate 3 lacks enough deep-loop positive markers, leaving 26 write-producing deep-research and deep-review prompts in no_match despite the advisor already identifying the correct loop owner.

Mixed prompts that start with review, inspect, audit, or explain but end in a concrete write tail still collapse under read_only_override. A narrow tail-write exception would recover 8 corpus rows without introducing false positives in the dry run.

The advisor emits command-only mirrors as top-1 labels even when the labeled corpus expects the owning skill. Normalizing command-memory-save and the command-spec-kit subcommands back to their owning skills improves exact-match accuracy from 53.5% to 60.0%.

Confidence-threshold tuning alone is not a useful first remediation lever. Lowering the threshold from 0.80 to 0.75 changes 2 prompts but does not improve exact-match accuracy, and raising it to 0.85 drops accuracy from 53.5% to 51.0%.

The 31 FF prompts cluster in mixed_ambiguous (19) and deep_loop_write (9), so the remaining double-failure surface is concentrated enough to target with explicit rule patches rather than broad classifier changes.
